evidence: Protocol and uploadsvc maintain separate action types/constants and parsers rather than one shared contract boundary (`goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` defines `Action` + `ActionUploadOpenEndpoint`/`ActionUploadReset`; uploadsvc tests refer to `uploadsvc.ActionUploadOpenEndpoint` and `uploadsvc.ParseAction`).

The adapter layer relies on string casting to bridge the two models (`uploadsvc.Action(action.Action)` and `cmd.Action != uploadsvc.Action(action.Action)`), which creates runtime coupling and hides type-level incompatibilities.
Supported action coverage is validated by test parity loops instead of a package-level shared contract export.
`Action` contract is spread across at least two modules (`protocol/actions` and uploadsvc) with mirrored names and overlapping semantics, so ownership of action semantics is unclear.

Cross-module alignment is enforced in tests through casts/parity checks rather than explicit API composition (for example, adapter tests iterate protocol action metas and assert translation into uploadsvc command actions).
There is no visible top-level canonical boundary that makes `protocol/actions` and uploadsvc compositionally coupled as intended for a stable domain capability.

Multiple listed files still carry stale/unused imports (e.g., `goseg/importer/importer.go` has unused `types/volume`, `gorilla/mux`, `zap`; `goseg/main.go` has unused `zap` and `gorilla/mux`; `goseg/routines/docker.go` has unused `events` and `zap`).
Bootstrap and runtime wiring files import broad dependency sets while exposing only a narrow surface, increasing compile-time coupling and making package drift harder to detect.
The seam layer (`goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go`) and orchestration wiring create additional dependency bridges instead of simplifying ownership boundaries.

Runtime-seam abstraction style is repeated across packages: `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go`, `goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go`, and `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` all bundle many collaborators in wide runtime objects.

`goseg/startup_orchestrator.go` and startup wiring in `goseg/main.go` coordinate a large number of background services through that shared graph in a single flow, making initialization ordering and failure semantics opaque.
This indicates duplicated seam patterns instead of a stable, low-friction boundary contract between modules.

`goseg/broadcast/collectors/collectors.go` is large and mechanically flagged as low-leverage decomposition (multiple disconnected function clusters), yet is used directly by the broadcast coordinator in `goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go`, suggesting blurred module boundaries.
In listed modules, several files are coupling setup, orchestration, and service actions in the same execution path instead of isolating cohesive operations into smaller, single-purpose functions.
goseg/startram/doc.go contains one short package comment focused on boundary responsibility and error policy.
goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go contains package-level guidance but no algorithmic code, logs, or wrapper boilerplate.
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: AI-debt signal is elevated by comment ratio tooling, largely because these are doc-only files; this may overstate debt if package docs are considered intentional documentation.

evidence: No DEPRECATED markers, TODO migration breadcrumbs, or legacy/new API coexistence text appears in goseg/startram/doc.go.
No migration stubs or legacy compatibility notes are present in goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go.
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: No actionable migration debt is visible in the reviewed package comments; completeness cannot be further validated from package docs alone.

evidence: Holistic structure reports `goseg/docker/orchestration/` as a 20-file production/test folder (11 production, 9 test), which crosses the "flat directory overload >10 files" threshold referenced by the package_organization rule set.

`container_config.go` in this directory is a small dispatch/factory module while `llama.go` is a long container-specialized builder that also writes local runtime script files and manages volumes/network bindings, showing mixed ownership under one folder.

This same directory is also expected to house unrelated concern slices (e.g., container configs plus lifecycle/status surfaces implied by structural scan and test split), increasing navigation and change-surface ambiguity.

`goseg/uploadsvc/service_test.go` repeatedly asserts fixed action-order expectations (`len(actions)==2`, `actions[0]==ActionUploadOpenEndpoint`, `actions[1]==ActionUploadReset`) instead of asserting set semantics for supported-action contracts.
No companion assertion in this batch shows that action ordering is part of the API contract; ordering appears to be an implementation detail, so tests are coupled to an un-declared detail.
`goseg/uploadsvc/service_test.go` also checks operation text through `DescribeAction`, but there is no order-agnostic normalization test for supported actions in the adapter-side contract tests.
test_strategy.issues_preventing_higher_score: Introduce order-agnostic supported-action assertions and add a contract test matrix that checks membership semantics across protocol/service/adapter, keeping order checks only when explicitly documented.

evidence: Multiple control-plane loops and orchestration paths are split across separate functions with different lifecycle/error-reporting models, which increases cohesion debt and increases the chance of partial-failure behavior.
Broadcast state is being assembled and exposed through multiple lock/unlock phases rather than as a single immutable snapshot handoff.
design_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: No single orchestration abstraction exists for lifecycle-bound long-running workers and state-assembly boundaries are not consistently atomic.

evidence: Function and type names across `goseg/config/config.go`, `goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go`, and `goseg/importer/importer.go` are generally intent-revealing (`InitializeConfig`, `BroadcastToClients`, `SetUploadSession`).
Cross-file contracts use mostly consistent domain terms (`session`, `auth`, `broadcast`, `runtime`).
naming_quality.issues_preventing_higher_score: Some naming still overloads 'token' semantics (ID vs full token value) across auth/importer paths, which affects clarity and discoverability of authorization contracts.

evidence: `goseg/leak/handler.go` has conditional noun handling that silently exits when decoded payload shape is unexpected, leaving behavior implicit instead of being explicitly rejected.
`goseg/importer/importer.go` performs staged validation across methods (`OpenUploadEndpoint` and later request validators) with mixed criteria, requiring readers to infer intended control flow.
logic_clarity.issues_preventing_higher_score: Mixed authorization and validation branches are spread across import/auth handling and leak message handling without shared explicit guard helpers, which lowers readability and traceability.

evidence: Type-based branching in `goseg/leak/handler.go` relies on runtime assumptions about noun structure; malformed shapes are not surfaced as typed failures.
Serialization/dispatch entrypoints generally avoid unchecked panics, but explicit typed error returns are inconsistent in malformed-path handling.
type_safety.issues_preventing_higher_score: Type guards in hot paths are not normalized through shared validators; adding explicit typed error objects for invalid request/message shapes would improve safety and diagnosability.

evidence: In `goseg/importer/importer.go`, `OpenUploadEndpoint` authorizes continuation using `IsTokenIdAuthed(token.ID)` (token-id only).
The same flow later validates session identity with `existingSession.Token != token` (full token comparison), creating a semantic split between auth acceptance and session matching.
contract_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: The token contract is effectively duplicated with different equality rules; there is no single canonical session-token validation API.

evidence: `goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go` defers to an async send path on channel saturation (`go func(){...}` fallback), which changes failure behavior between synchronous and asynchronous paths.
Malformed/unsupported leak payloads in `goseg/leak/handler.go` are not returned as explicit protocol errors, so callers receive no uniform failure signal.
error_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Error policy is inconsistent across modules (sync validation failures, async drops/retry semantics, silent drops), requiring a shared response/error contract.

evidence: Global/runtime setup in `goseg/startup_orchestrator.go` and `goseg/main.go` wires many subsystems in one path, including channels, background services, and upload/auth configuration.
Subsequent code in workflow/adapter modules assumes these globals are already initialized, raising implicit startup ordering dependence.
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: A clearer dependency graph (explicit runtime/input requirements per module) would reduce implicit coupling during startup and in tests.

evidence: Across `goseg/main.go`, `goseg/startup_orchestrator.go`, and lifecycle/config modules, startup/validation responsibilities are separated using a mostly consistent pattern.
No major style or naming outliers are pervasive in the reviewed files.
convention_outlier.issues_preventing_higher_score: Outliers remain in token/session naming and validation semantics, which are cross-cutting rather than localized convention issues.

evidence: Auth/token lifecycle for upload flows spans `goseg/importer/importer.go` and `goseg/auth/auth.go` with separate guard functions and assumptions.
API surface behavior (what is required to be valid for session continuity) is not consistently documented in one place or enforced by one gate.
api_surface_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: No single request-auth contract object exists for upload/session operations; callers rely on inferred behavior across files.

evidence: `goseg/importer/importer.go` first accepts session continuation by token ID (`IsTokenIdAuthed(token.ID)`), then later performs stricter full-token comparison (`existingSession.Token != token`), creating two inconsistent authorization interpretations in one flow.
This same endpoint also invokes `auth.ValidateAndAuthorizeRequestToken` in follow-on validation, duplicating auth checks.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Authorization semantics should be unified behind one validator returning a clear decision mode/state; current split allows drift in edge cases.

findings.summary: Transport action contracts are duplicated across protocol and uploadsvc, creating a hidden drift risk at module boundaries.
evidence: protocol/actions defines its own action enum/type (`ActionUploadOpenEndpoint`, `ActionUploadReset`) and parsing helpers.
uploadsvc tests and adapters still rely on local uploadsvc action symbols and translate from protocol symbols with `uploadsvc.Action(action.Action)` casts.
No shared exported constant/type boundary is used between packages in the listed files, so contract consistency depends on test discipline rather than compile-time structure.
findings.suggestion: Establish a single source of truth for upload actions (e.g., canonical `protocol.Action` as the exported wire contract and reuse it in uploadsvc), or explicitly re-export protocol action constants from uploadsvc via adapter functions so the two packages cannot drift independently.

findings.summary: Cross-module action alignment is represented by parity tests and implicit casts instead of clear module ownership.
evidence: Adapter tests call `actions.ActionMetas(actions.NamespaceUpload)` and then assert they map into uploadsvc `cmd.Action` by value casting, so behavior coupling is implicit and spread across tests.
service tests assert exact action slice structure/order from uploadsvc (`len(actions) == 2`, `actions[0] == ...`), which entangles test assumptions with ordering rather than intent.
There is no visible boundary module that advertises the action contract as the authoritative domain exchange format.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit exported protocol contract metadata (e.g., descriptor map with action semantics) and make adapters consume that single surface directly; make tests assert map-keyed compatibility and reject order-dependent checks.

findings.summary: Dead imports across execution-path files indicate accumulated dependency drift and unnecessary package coupling.
evidence: `goseg/importer/importer.go` retains imports (`types/volume`, `gorilla/mux`, `zap`) reported as unused.
`goseg/main.go` reports unused imports for `zap` and `gorilla/mux` while serving orchestration/bootstrap responsibilities.
`goseg/routines/docker.go` still has unused imports in a file that already sits near orchestration seams, amplifying graph noise.
These stale dependencies are across core runtime and orchestration paths, not isolated test-only files.
findings.suggestion: Remove stale imports and split seam-specific packages so each file depends only on the abstractions it actually calls; if deferred usage is planned, gate imports by build tags or defer code behind explicit optional compile paths.

findings.summary: Repeated wide dependency-bag seams force modules to absorb each other’s implementation surface.
evidence: The same broad pattern appears across multiple files: runtime structs composed as large function/interface bundles passed through layers.
Callers import these broad runtime containers and access many capabilities that are unrelated to their local behavior.
This produces repetitive constructor signatures and brittle handoff code rather than explicit, narrow contracts.
findings.suggestion: Replace the shared, cross-module runtime bags with narrow interfaces for each use-case (e.g., container ops, wireguard ops, workflow ops), and inject only those interfaces at each boundary.

findings.summary: Startup logic is centralized in orchestrator modules with high coupling and implicit ordering assumptions.
evidence: Startup/bootstrap flow spans `main.go` and `startup_orchestrator.go` and drives many background routines through one orchestration path.
The startup sequence embeds service creation and launch policy in one place, making dependency ordering and recoverability difficult to reason about.
Runtime seam objects are treated as a single handoff payload instead of explicit subsystem interfaces.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a declarative startup plan with explicit service dependency metadata and health/error policy, so subsystem startup is registered and ordered by contract rather than manually coupled in one function.

findings.summary: Collector and broadcast responsibilities are bundled together, making behavior harder to isolate and test incrementally.
evidence: `goseg/broadcast/collectors/collectors.go` is large and marked with disconnected function clusters, indicating mixed concerns under one module.
The broadcast coordinator directly depends on that broad collector module while additional broadcast/leak adapters also consume it, increasing coupling among unrelated paths.
Tests can only mock whole collector/orchestration interactions rather than narrow collector contracts.
findings.suggestion: Split collectors into focused modules (eg. source/event/filtering/state collectors) with narrow interfaces, and inject only required collector contracts into broadcast/leak entrypoints.

findings.summary: goseg/docker/orchestration is overloaded and mixes container-type factories with broader orchestration responsibilities.
evidence: The directory-level evidence already shows 20 files in `goseg/docker/orchestration`, exceeding flat-directory threshold and indicating this folder is now a mixed boundary.

`container_config.go` is a dispatch module (`ContainerConfigForType`) while `llama.go` embeds container-specialized behavior and startup-file creation, so naming and role cohesion diverge within the same package.
`docker_test.go` and related runtime tests sit in the same package root, so intent-based ownership is not represented by directory structure.
findings.suggestion: Create domain buckets under `goseg/docker/orchestration/` (recommended order): 1) add `goseg/docker/orchestration/config/` and move `container_config.go` plus each container-specific config module (`llama.go` etc.) into focused files like `container_type.go`, `minio.go`, `wireguard.go` already by concern; 2) add `goseg/docker/orchestration/events/` for event/listener and transition orchestration; 3) adjust package imports and test locations so each subpackage has colocated tests. Then run: `gofmt -w goseg/docker/orchestration/**/*.go`, `go test ./goseg/docker/orchestration/...`, `go test ./goseg/...`.

findings.summary: Docker lifecycle orchestration is split into a generic routines package instead of a dedicated docker orchestration boundary.
evidence: `routines/docker.go` owns end-to-end docker orchestration flow (`StartDockerSubsystem`, `dockerListenerWithContext`, `dockerSubscriptionHandler`, `runDockerTransitionWorkflow`, `check502Loop`) rather than simple routine primitives.
The file also wires broadcast/recovery runtime adapters and restart decisions, which ties it to docker subsystem ownership rather than generic scheduling utilities.
Related routine monitor files in the same package use long-running loop ownership patterns, increasing risk that this package becomes an unstructured catch-all for subsystem responsibilities.
findings.suggestion: Introduce `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/` (or similar) for lifecycle orchestration and event-driven state transitions currently in `routines/docker.go`, and keep `goseg/routines/` to only host truly generic loop wrappers. Suggested sequence: 1) move `StartDockerSubsystem`, `Check502Loop`, and transition workflow wiring to new package; 2) preserve exported surface via a small facade (`routines/docker` calling facade) to avoid call-site churn; 3) relocate affected tests into the new package and keep only contract tests in `routines`; 4) update imports and run `go test ./goseg/routines/...` and `go test ./goseg/docker/orchestration/...`.

findings.summary: Startram backup runtime combines transport, crypto, filesystem, and restore orchestration in one module boundary.
evidence: The same runtime module performs backup API requests, encryption/decryption, archive/volume write logic, and restore orchestration control flow.

`backup_service.go` defines a service abstraction (`BackupInfrastructureService`) and facade methods, while `backup_runtime.go` still carries cross-cutting implementation work, so ownership between service boundary and execution body is blurred.
Tests for restore and transport paths indicate these mixed concerns are currently coupled in a single package surface.
findings.suggestion: Split `startram` backup concerns into explicit layers: 1) `goseg/startram/backup/transport` for API URL/client and DTO fetch/upload plumbing (`getBackup`, `uploadBackup`); 2) `goseg/startram/backup/crypto` for `encryptFile`/`decryptFile`; 3) `goseg/startram/backup/restore` for restore orchestration and volume write flow (`restoreBackupProd`, `writeBackupToVolume`); 4) keep `backup_service.go` as the orchestration facade delegating to these packages. Move and align tests by concern, then run `gofmt -w goseg/startram/backup/**/*.go` and `go test ./goseg/startram/...`.

findings.summary: Supported-action tests assert slice indexes, coupling test expectations to non-contractual ordering.
evidence: `goseg/uploadsvc/service_test.go` asserts `actions[0] == ActionUploadOpenEndpoint` and `actions[1] == ActionUploadReset` in multiple places.
`goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` builds action lists from internal slice literals without declaring order as API contract, so test ordering assertions are stronger than contract guarantees.
No test in this batch explicitly documents action ordering as contract-critical.
findings.suggestion: Convert order-sensitive assertions to order-agnostic set checks in `goseg/uploadsvc/service_test.go` (e.g., map/set or sort+compare), and keep index assertions only if order is explicitly part of API shape. Add a focused contract test documenting whether order is stable and why.

findings.summary: Docker health watcher starts unbounded background loops with no cancellation path, creating lifecycle asymmetry.
evidence: `StartDockerHealthLoops` starts `Check502Loop` in a goroutine with no context argument and no stop signal.
`Check502Loop` calls `check502Loop(newDockerRoutineRuntime())`, which uses an infinite `for { ... }` loop and never observes parent shutdown state.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a context/stop channel into `check502Loop` and `Check502Loop`, pass the same context from `StartDockerSubsystem`, and guard startup to prevent duplicate loops when bootstrap is reentered.

findings.summary: Broadcast bootstrap publishes partially initialized state across multiple lock windows.
evidence: `bootstrapBroadcastState` updates `broadcastState` under multiple short lock blocks (`broadcastState.Urbits`, then `System`, then `Profile`, then `Apps`).
The same function starts `BroadcastLoop` after some fields are written, so readers can observe mixed-state combinations under concurrent access.
`ConstructPierInfo`, `constructSystemInfo`, and `constructProfileInfo` are computed separately and composed piecemeal instead of as a single snapshot.
findings.suggestion: Build a complete `structs.AuthBroadcast` snapshot locally, then acquire `mu` once and replace state atomically; only after replacement should the background broadcast loop begin.

findings.summary: Startram recovery and transition handlers duplicate orchestration/error-aggregation patterns instead of sharing a runbook abstraction.
evidence: `shipworkflow/startram.go` and `shipworkflow/recovery.go` both implement near-identical ordered step execution with manual `[]error` accumulation and wrapped context strings.
`appendOrchestrationError` and `appendWireguardRecoveryError` are functionally the same pattern (contextual wrap + append + log), duplicated by intent.
`urbit_operations.go` contains additional operation-specific transition orchestration with direct container/config side effects, increasing the same pattern across ship workflows.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared ship/orchestration runbook helper in `shipworkflow` that executes named steps, normalizes transition emission, and centralizes aggregated error handling, then reuse it across recovery, restart, and toggle paths.

findings.summary: Upload session authorization uses different token semantics in the same request lifecycle, so acceptance can differ between steps.
evidence: `OpenUploadEndpoint` checks `auth.IsTokenIdAuthed(token.ID)` (ID-only) before session setup.
Downstream path compares persisted `existingSession.Token` against the full `token` value.
The importer layer also calls `auth.ValidateAndAuthorizeRequestToken` in follow-on validation.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single canonical upload-session auth contract (e.g., a `ValidateUploadSessionToken(session, requestToken)` helper) that defines one equivalence rule and one error outcome, then replace split checks in importer and related handlers with that helper.

findings.summary: Token authorization is checked in multiple places with non-equivalent conditions, creating inconsistent pass/fail outcomes.
evidence: ID-based pass in `IsTokenIdAuthed(token.ID)` and full-token equality check for session reuse use different acceptance criteria.
`validateUploadRequest` adds yet another revalidation call, duplicating auth work without shared policy.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate authorization policy into a dedicated strategy in `auth` and consume it from importer entrypoints; return a typed authorization result that indicates exactly why a token is invalid or rotated.

findings.summary: Malformed leak payload shapes can be dropped without explicit error signaling, reducing debuggability and protocol clarity.
evidence: In handler decode/dispatch flow, payload type branching assumes cell-shaped noun and does not emit a protocol error when shape assertions fail.
Control returns without structured feedback to caller path for invalid message shapes.
findings.suggestion: Add explicit validation/error branch for non-cell payloads that returns a structured protocol error event, and add table-driven tests in leak tests to cover malformed noun inputs.
